text:
$FN bombed in after hours  -11%

"Photonics"

Some land mines appearing.  Let's see how these open tomorrow and most importantly how they close in coming few days. 

If they break the 21 ema and don't recover they tend to be lost for a while https://t.co/AtUouW9KGU

text:
Biggest moves come from biggest stories.

- memory supercycle
- gov't stake in INTC
- photonics bottleneck
- war = big oil move
- software short (anthropic narrative)
+ plenty more

Some new(er) "stories" I'm watching:
- live commerce
- tokenization of assets
- new drone use-cases
- personalized health
- ai agents

Price *usually* shapes the narrative...but sometimes you can sniff out the future narratives before price even realizes what's happening.

I have followers across many industries, what are you guys sniffing out that nobody else realizes yet?

This Swedish micro-cap operates in two powerful verticals — Photonics (DFB lasers, optical semiconductors for fiber networks) and Wireless (5G mmWave RF chips) — and just landed a major catalyst. Sivers announced a collaboration with Jabil to develop a 1.6T linear receive optical (LRO) transceiver module using their high-performance Distributed Feedback (DFB) lasers, targeting next-generation hyperscale AI data centers. ￼ The module is designed to reduce energy consumption up to 2.5x compared with existing solutions ￼ — a hard requirement as hyperscalers fight power constraints. 800G and higher speed optical transceivers are projected to make up 80% of the pluggables market by 2030, growing to 225M units shipped globally. ￼ Revenue is already moving — 2025 revenue grew 32.91% year-over-year ￼ — and the stock has delivered nearly a 6x return over the past year as investors rapidly reprice its growth potential. ￼ OTC-listed, thinly traded, and volatile — but if you’re tracking the photonics supply chain for AI, Sivers deserves to be on your watchlist.\nNot financial advice."}}}, "rest_id": "2046892456625471815", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "19731", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2051441014171226566", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "16310", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Everything looks to be going to plan. Another situation with these photonics stocks where listening to management talk is way more important than the numbers. This is a forward growth story about becoming "a top 3 global laser supplier" with the ramp not starting until H2. The company raised full year guidance to $1.1B from $1B. Talked about their 900,00 sq. ft in U.S. factory space doing up to 30% production in USA on top of their 700K+ Taiwanese space Discussed their CPO laser which they plan to 80x production of (said they're in talks with several customers on this) Said margins could get to 40% towards end of year up from ~30% Working with 3 hyperscalers, one qualified, another almost qualified. Lin said real demand is $1.4-$1.5B even though they can only fulfill $1.1B. The company re-affirmed their target of hitting $450 monthly revenue in June/July 2027 which would imply an annual revenue run rate of $5B+ at a current market cap of ~$12B. This is what has triggered the massive re-rate. Execution risk remains crazy high but management just doubled down on what they previously said which is why the stock trade as high as $190 recently. I just don't think the strategically important infrastructure of AAOI (domestic laser fabs, vertical integration, CPO laser) is being given enough credit here. This could easily still be a multi-bagger if they execute, with the possibility of being worth several tens of billions in the next few years hence why it's the largest position in my photonics fund."}}}, "rest_id": "2052509803302555865", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "41105", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2052528433226526947", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "69", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
COHR may be one of the most important AI infrastructure companies most investors still don’t fully understand.

Not GPUs. Not ASICs. Not memory.

Photonics.

Coherent sits at the physical layer of the AI buildout — the optical spine connecting GPUs, switches, and data centers.

Q3 FY26 revenue hit a record $1.81B, datacenter revenue grew 37% YoY, and NVIDIA just wrote a $2B check to secure the roadmap.

The bull case: 6-inch InP fabs, 800G → 1.6T → CPO, and a $23B growth SAM.

The bear case: debt, valuation, and execution risk.

Full deep dive below.

"to_index": 3582}, {"from_index": 3651, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 3685}]}, "text": "When you follow $SIVE closely over time, one thing that stands out is how many parallel tracks the company is advancing simultaneously. It rarely feels like a story built around a single product or market. Instead, it is a platform company where the same core photonics technology is applied across large and growing domains. One update that highlighted this breadth came on March 11, 2026, when $SIVE announced that a strategic LiDAR customer had chosen to integrate its photonics technology across all platforms. Production ramp is scheduled to begin in Q4 2026, targeting automotive and industrial applications. This is not a limited component agreement. $SIVE is supplying high-power continuous wave DFB laser diode arrays together with optical amplifiers integrated throughout the customer’s portfolio. Estimated cumulative lifetime revenue potential is $53–138 million, based on known volumes today, with clear upside as the customer scales. All indications point to this customer being $AEVA. Their 4D FMCW LiDAR technology, used in Atlas and X1, is engineered for long range, precise velocity measurement, and high resolution — capabilities required for real-world autonomous systems where safety and reliability are critical. $SIVE’s lasers help enable that performance, and their implementation across all platforms demonstrates the strength of $SIVE’s III-V photonics platform. It is worth stepping back and reflecting on what this means. The same technology stack powering highly efficient 1.6T transceivers with Jabil for AI data center infrastructure is now also finding its way into LiDAR sensors for trucks, passenger vehicles, robotics, and industrial automation. This ability to leverage one core competence across end-markets is one of the most compelling aspects of the $SIVE investment case. It lowers development risk, creates multiple growth engines, and allows ramps to mature at their own pace. $AEVA already has notable commercial momentum. They are integrated into $NVDA DRIVE Hyperion with volume production targeted for 2028 and have a partnership with Daimler Truck. As more OEMs, fleet operators, and robotics companies adopt similar LiDAR solutions, each new design win or deployment can drive higher shipment volumes. Automotive platforms often run for 8–10 years or longer, while industrial and robotics applications can create more stable long-term revenue streams. That gives $SIVE substantial room to grow beyond today’s base-case estimate. In the current market discussion, much focus is understandably on nearer-term opportunities — FWA and mmWave with Tachyon, or SATCOM progress through https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp and $YSS. Those areas are important and may generate revenue momentum earlier. But the LiDAR ramp is a meaningful and underappreciated building block for 2027 and beyond. It does not begin as a small proof-of-concept. It starts with a strategic customer embedding $SIVE technology deeply from the beginning. This kind of multi-domain execution is what gives $SIVE its resilience and long-term potential. Photonics is the common thread connecting AI infrastructure (CPO), wireless connectivity (mmWave/FWA), satellite communications, and autonomous perception systems (LiDAR). With a reported pipeline exceeding $453 million and several parallel ramps in different stages of maturity, the company is not overly dependent on any single market or customer. The LiDAR opportunity still feels underappreciated. It may not dominate the daily discussion, but it starts contributing real production volumes already in Q4 this year. And as $AEVA expands in automotive and industrial markets, $SIVE’s participation across all platforms positions the company to capture a growing share of that opportunity. It is a reminder of why the longer-term thesis around $SIVE remains compelling: a specialized photonics capability that can scale across multiple high-growth verticals, creating revenue streams with significant long-term compounding potential."}}}, "rest_id": "2052353159398392187", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "524368", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2052515063945199683", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3114", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

PC에서 파워가 전력을 만들고, 메인보드·그래픽카드·RAM이 그 전력을 나눠 쓰듯이, 포토닉스 링크에서는 Laser가 만든 Optical Power가 Coupler → Waveguide → Modulator → Waveguide → PD로 이동한다. 문제는 이 과정에서 계속 Loss가 발생한다는 것이다. 보통 Laser Power는 mW가 아니라 dBm으로 본다. 쉽게 말하면, 100mW = 20 dBm 400mW = 26 dBm 이다. 400mW Laser는 100mW보다 출력은 4배 강하지만, dBm 기준으로는 +6 dB 높은 Laser다. 예를 들어 Laser 출력이 10 dBm이라고 해보자. Coupler에서 2 dB, Waveguide에서 2 dB, Modulator에서 3 dB 손실이 나면, PD에 도착하는 Optical Power는 결국 3 dBm 수준이다. 즉, 앞에서 아무리 강한 Laser를 넣어도 중간 Loss가 크면 PD가 받아들이는 Power는 크게 줄어든다. 반대로 말하면, Laser 자체가 무조건 강해야 좋은 시스템이 되는 것도 아니다. Laser에서 PD까지 가는 경로의 Loss가 낮으면, 더 낮은 Laser Power로도 충분히 쓸 수 있다. 그래서 포토닉스에서 중요한 질문은 단순히 “Laser가 얼마나 세냐?”가 아니다. 진짜 핵심은 이거다. 그 Laser Power를 PD까지 얼마나 덜 잃고 전달하느냐. 결국 Photonics는 Laser 싸움이면서 동시에 dB Loss 싸움이다."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2051926679506088437"}}, "rest_id": "2052017338346885248", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "48248", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2052513391898739130", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "753", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Anyone else grab $AAOI in the 130's?

Everything looks to be going to plan.

Another situation with these photonics stocks where listening to management talk is way more important than the numbers.

This is a forward growth story about becoming "a top 3 global laser supplier" with the ramp not starting until H2.

The company raised full year guidance to $1.1B from $1B.
Talked about their 900,00 sq. ft in U.S. factory space doing up to 30% production in USA on top of their 700K+ Taiwanese space
Discussed their CPO laser which they plan to 80x production of (said they're in talks with several customers on this)
Said margins could get to 40% towards end of year up from ~30%
Working with 3 hyperscalers, one qualified, another almost qualified.
Lin said real demand is $1.4-$1.5B even though they can only fulfill $1.1B.

The company re-affirmed their target of hitting $450 monthly revenue in June/July 2027 which would imply an annual revenue run rate of $5B+ at a current market cap of ~$12B. This is what has triggered the massive re-rate.

Execution risk remains crazy high but management just doubled down on what they previously said which is why the stock trade as high as $190 recently. 

I just don't think the strategically important infrastructure of AAOI (domestic laser fabs, vertical integration, CPO laser) is being given enough credit here. 

This could easily still be a multi-bagger if they execute, with the possibility of being worth several tens of billions in the next few years hence why it's the largest position in my photonics fund.

5. 반면 CW 레이저 기반의 SiPh 솔루션은 이미 양산 사이클 안에 있음. 

$AAOI 는 2025년 말에 400mW 초고출력 협대역 CW 레이저를 출시했고

이는 하이퍼스케일러의 Silicon Photonics 및 CPO 아키텍처를 직접 겨냥한 제품임.  

800G 램프가 2026년 본격화되고, 1.6T는 2026년 후반 양산 진입을 목표로 하고 있음.

text:
$SITM my second blockbuster this season, after $STRL.
25% up on earnings!!!

I was a bit nervous because of the cost of Renesas acquisition in Feb. 

All the photonics chasers in X miss out this gem. I don't know how and why.

High precision clock is very important for synchronization in any digital communication -- between logic units (GPUs, CPUs, TPUs) for parallel computation and conversion of electrical signals to optical signals. That's right. There is a unit called DSP (digital signal processor) which takes binary electrical data from the logic units and transforms it into an electrical signal for light modulation unit which chops the CW light. For 800G/1.6T to work, it requires to handle sub-70 femtosecond jitter. Although $SWKS can achieve this, but in a convoluted way. $SITM's MEMS Resonators are tiny and work solid at 100C. Ideal for optical tranceivers and CPOs.

Another huge positive story is Renesas integration, where $SITM can vertically integrate this crucial part of the photonic fabrics. Instead of having a separate clock chip on a board, Renesas' microcontroller unit can have $SITM's clock integrated inside the package. With this, $SITM will be able to distribute the timing signal, clean jitters for 800G/1.6T networking, synchronize 5G/6G base stations. It will be unbeatable!
embedded_text:
$SITM is creating a very wide moat around high speed MEMS, I think.

EpiSeals, DualMEMS, plua 100+ patents owned by Luts
Now, adding all IPs from Renesas

Laser, Photonics, ... without ultrafast clocking doesn't make much sense for sure.

https://t.co/l63fFJHlP1

text:
If you invest in photonics, please read this.

I spent this weekend researching the OCS supply chain.

Quick lesson:

> OCS = Optical Circuit Switching
> Hyperscalers want lower power, lower latency, and faster AI networks
> OCS helps by keeping light as light
> Instead of optical → electrical → optical conversion at every hop, the signal can stay optical through the switch

That is why this is becoming a real theme.

$LITE already has a multi-year, multi-billi on-dollar OCS purchase agreement.

Management called OCS the “biggest single tightrope” the company is walking because customers want output faster than the supply chain can ramp.

$COHR doubled its OCS market opportunity estimate to over $4B.

They also said they fixed a production bottleneck and are now ramping output across two facilities.

$HUBN has Polatis, one of the more hidden OCS assets in public markets.

They secured major hyperscale OCS orders and opened a new Poland facility to increase capacity at least 5x over two years.

This is still early.

There is also an entire supply chain here.

So I built a public OCS map with 12 companies worth knowing.

I currently own 4 of them.

I want to own 2 more.

Read it here:

https://t.co/97bLrkIvE8
embedded_text:
I think OCS is going to be a massive revenue driver
$LITE $COHR

But Lumentum and Coherent aren't the only ways to play

I just posted about a company that is working on taking share in this market

It is one that I don't hear often, but they are an established player

text:
$IPGP IPG Photonics: The Strategic Pivot, Path to Recovery, and Investment Thesis.

IPG Photonics is the dominant player in fiber lasers, but dominance in industrial cutting has become a more complicated position to hold. Chinese competitors have driven sustained price pressure in that segment, and the margin profile that once defined IPG's quality premium has eroded as a result. The strategic response — pivoting toward medical technology, battery welding, and defense — is the right directional move, but it's an early-stage reorientation against a backdrop of near-term financial pressure.

Recent results show demand recovering and revenue growing, which confirms that the core business remains viable. The more important question is whether that top-line recovery translates into margin restoration, and the answer so far is incomplete. Free cash flow conversion has been inconsistent, and profitability hasn't yet reflected the volume improvement in a way that validates the thesis.

Leadership has been overhauled. The new CEO is focused on operational discipline and broadening the product portfolio toward integrated laser systems — a higher-value configuration that moves IPG up the stack from component supplier to solutions provider. That's the right ambition, but integrated systems require different sales motions, longer customer relationships, and more complex delivery capabilities than standalone laser units. The organizational transition to support that shift is still underway.

The valuation is carrying a recovery premium that the financials haven't yet earned. The stock is pricing in a successful execution of the strategic pivot, which means margin expansion and free cash flow improvement need to materialize on a visible timeline to justify current levels. A high-quality business with a credible long-term direction, but the entry point demands that the complex pieces — new leadership, new markets, new product configurations — come together with reasonable speed.

The upside is real if the pivot lands. Medical, battery, and defense are structurally better markets than commodity industrial cutting, and IPG's technical foundation is strong enough to compete credibly in all three. The risk is execution timeline — the gap between strategic intent and financial proof points is still wide, and the stock doesn't offer much patience buffer at current multiples.

IPG Photonics is the dominant player in fiber lasers, but dominance in industrial cutting has become a more complicated position to hold. Chinese competitors have driven sustained price pressure in that segment, and the margin profile that once defined IPG's quality premium has eroded as a result. The strategic response — pivoting toward medical technology, battery welding, and defense — is the right directional move, but it's an early-stage reorientation against a backdrop of near-term financial pressure. Recent results show demand recovering and revenue growing, which confirms that the core business remains viable. The more important question is whether that top-line recovery translates into margin restoration, and the answer so far is incomplete. Free cash flow conversion has been inconsistent, and profitability hasn't yet reflected the volume improvement in a way that validates the thesis. Leadership has been overhauled. The new CEO is focused on operational discipline and broadening the product portfolio toward integrated laser systems — a higher-value configuration that moves IPG up the stack from component supplier to solutions provider. That's the right ambition, but integrated systems require different sales motions, longer customer relationships, and more complex delivery capabilities than standalone laser units. The organizational transition to support that shift is still underway. The valuation is carrying a recovery premium that the financials haven't yet earned. The stock is pricing in a successful execution of the strategic pivot, which means margin expansion and free cash flow improvement need to materialize on a visible timeline to justify current levels. A high-quality business with a credible long-term direction, but the entry point demands that the complex pieces — new leadership, new markets, new product configurations — come together with reasonable speed. The upside is real if the pivot lands. Medical, battery, and defense are structurally better markets than commodity industrial cutting, and IPG's technical foundation is strong enough to compete credibly in all three. The risk is execution timeline — the gap between strategic intent and financial proof points is still wide, and the stock doesn't offer much patience buffer at current multiples."}}}, "quoted_status_result": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "article": {"article_results": {"result": {"cover_media": {"id": "QXBpTWVkaWE6DAAFCgABHH/dDWBW0AIKAAIZgoybDlbAAAAA", "media_id": "2053603004624850946", "media_info": {"__typename": "ApiImage", "color_info": {"palette": [{"percentage": 88.27, "rgb": {"blue": 254, "green": 254, "red": 254}}, {"percentage": 8.87, "rgb": {"blue": 98, "green": 50, "red": 26}}, {"percentage": 2.73, "rgb": {"blue": 157, "green": 128, "red": 116}}, {"percentage": 0.14, "rgb": {"blue": 172, "green": 147, "red": 135}}]}, "original_img_height": 793, "original_img_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/media/HH_dDWBW0AIzX4q.jpg", "original_img_width": 1983}, "media_key": "3_2053603004624850946"}, "id": "QXJ0aWNsZUVudGl0eToyMDUzNjAyNjEwMTM4MjEwMzA0", "lifecycle_state": {"modified_at_secs": 1778454557}, "metadata": {"first_published_at_secs": 1778454557}, "preview_text": "EXECUTIVE OVERVIEW: IPG Photonics is no longer a simple high-power industrial cutting laser story. Combined value: ~8 billion SEK. https://t.co/k9EMCinhs5 It is framed as these investors are driving this on speculation. Yes maybe, that is of course possible. Or is there another angle? My take, SEK8B..what? Hence my reaction would probably be the opposite, this could be worth investigating: What thesis are these allocators actually trading? Is it pure speculation with SEK 8B in play? Who sits on these SEK8B? EFN however have asked "Richard Schatz, senior researcher in photonics at RISE Research Institutes of Sweden and former consultant for Sivers", for his opinion about Sivers. So what did Mr. Richard Schatz say: "There are several companies that manufacture these kinds of lasers, and Sivers is far from alone. I don't understand why the company has become so hyped. There are many players, not just in Europe and the US, but also in China," says Schatz. Honestly I have been COO and CEO at Sivers for almost 10 years 2015-2024 (2015 we had 18 headcount I known everyone in the Company until 2024) and also ask Board members that was in the board many years before me, both for Sivers and former CST Global (Sivers Photonics today) and none of them can recollect Mr. Schatz. I also sent an email, asking the reporter that interviewed Mr S and asked when he claims to have been a consultant at Sivers. Without reply. This doesn't really matter, it is just shows where the digging stops. Sivers Semiconductors is one of a handful of merchant InP laser suppliers globally. They have disclosed partnerships with POET Technologies, Jabil (1.6T LRO), O-Net, Ayar Labs and operate in the External Light Source category that scale-up CPO architectures depend on. Either are all these US investors dramatically wrong about the photonics layer of AI infrastructure, or the institutional allocators routing through US brokerages have identified what most Swedish investors haven't yet. Maybe this is worth investigating which? To be 100% clear no one knows (not even me), who has the right hypothesis, that we might know in 2028-2030 time frame. To help anyone intressted, I made this free and open substack to explain how the money flows. As they said in Washington follow the money... text:
This is something I didn't discuss back then, but it is essential knowledge

To understand $SIVE, we must recognize that two distinct branches exist:

-InP Lasers for Photonics (CPO Ramp up)

This is the segment currently catapulting the stock price

-MmWave Technology (Wireless)

In this branch, $SIVE possesses Radio Frequency (RF) technology capable of transmitting data over the air at ultra high speeds over short distances

Essentially, they manufacture chips and antennas for 5G, 6G, and Satellites (Satcom)

This is where the partnership with Tachyon Networks comes in developing 60GHz transceivers for $1.5M

Following a previous $3M deal for 28GHz transceivers

These technologies have reached a critical milestone

They are in the mass production phase with recurring orders

This is vital because while technologies like those from $ALMU or $KOPN are very promising, they are still far from this specific phase

The phase that often becomes a bottleneck and provides true vertical integration into the market

$SIVE’s wireless branch, however, is mature

While this second branch might seem less "flashy", it carries significant weight

Regarding the current revaluation of $SIVE, it is crucial as these figures are reflected in the earnings reports

Poor performance in this segment would tarnish the InP Laser branch, which is what the market cares about most right now

Furthermore, the fact that they are already operating at 28GHz and 60GHz proves that their silicon (InP) is reliable under real world conditions

Something that Photonics (CPO) customers examine under a magnifying glass before signing any contracts

text:
@UCF Thank you for holding the graduation ceremony last night, 5/9/26.  
I am so very proud of Joshua’s accomplishments on working hard to attain a Bachelor of Science degree in the field of Optics and Photonics.  He is a bright young man.  
Good luck in Delaware with the your next chapter of life.
I love you, Mom. 
#JoshuaLHendry

user_name: ゆるっと半導体｜AI・半導体ニュースを毎朝やさしく解説
text:
『Scintil Photonicsって、何してる会社？』 光集積回路（光信号を処理するチップ）の設計スタートアップなんだ。CEOのMatt Crowleyは物理学者出身で、研究室の技術を実際のものづくりに変える専門家。

2026年の今、Coherent・Lumentumといった光トランシーバー大手がCPO（共栄接光学）チップの増産に追われてるんだけど、こういう新興企業が『次の世代の回路設計』で割り込むパターンが増えてるんだ。

NVIDIAのBlackwell Ultra向け光学接続ケーブルの需要が膨らむなか、既存メーカーだけでは賄えない領域が出てきてるんだよ。Matt Crowleyのような『研究から量産への翻訳人』が活躍する土壌ができてる、ってのが今の市場の面白さなんだ。

→ AI時代の半導体投資では、有名大手より『こっそり成長する周辺企業』を先に見つけた人が勝つんだ。

text:
European and Asian Firms Express Strong Interest in Stakes in Canada’s Photonics Fabrication Centre Spin-Out 

The Government of Canada, through Industry Minister Joly and the National Research Council of Canada (NRC), is advancing the spin-out of the Canadian Photonics Fabrication Centre (CPFC)—North America’s only end-to-end pure-play compound semiconductor wafer fabrication facility—into a commercially oriented entity. The move, first signaled in Budget 2025 and formally announced in early May 2026, aims to attract private-sector capital to scale operations, expand Canada’s photonic semiconductor supply chain, and accelerate innovation in AI data centres, quantum computing, fibre-optic networks, and advanced technologies.

Key Context and Objectives

Strategic repositioning: Transition the publicly owned CPFC from a research-focused facility to a scaled commercial operation while maintaining majority Canadian ownership and control. The government will retain a stake, ensuring the entity remains anchored in Canada and continues to create high-skilled jobs.

Investment attraction: A formal call for proposals has been issued to secure private investment from “trusted partners.” The goal is to enhance domestic manufacturing capacity, support Canadian SMEs, and strengthen supply-chain resilience in critical photonics technologies.

Global interest: Minister Joly confirmed that firms from Europe and Asia have already expressed keen interest in taking stakes or investing in the facility. She highlighted opportunities to build a “middle-power alliance” by deepening ties with these partners, complementing North American demand.

Impact

This initiative positions Canada as a leader in photonic semiconductors—a foundational technology for AI, quantum, and high-performance computing—at a time of global supply-chain pressures. By leveraging international capital while safeguarding Canadian sovereignty, the spin-out is expected to drive economic growth, productivity, and technological sovereignty. Canadian companies such as Ranovus have already signaled intent to participate as investors or operators.

Firms Express Strong Interest in Stakes in Canada’s Photonics Fabrication Centre Spin-Out The Government of Canada, through Industry Minister Joly and the National Research Council of Canada (NRC), is advancing the spin-out of the Canadian Photonics Fabrication Centre (CPFC)—North America’s only end-to-end pure-play compound semiconductor wafer fabrication facility—into a commercially oriented entity. The move, first signaled in Budget 2025 and formally announced in early May 2026, aims to attract private-sector capital to scale operations, expand Canada’s photonic semiconductor supply chain, and accelerate innovation in AI data centres, quantum computing, fibre-optic networks, and advanced technologies. Key Context and Objectives Strategic repositioning: Transition the publicly owned CPFC from a research-focused facility to a scaled commercial operation while maintaining majority Canadian ownership and control. The government will retain a stake, ensuring the entity remains anchored in Canada and continues to create high-skilled jobs. Investment attraction: A formal call for proposals has been issued to secure private investment from “trusted partners.” The goal is to enhance domestic manufacturing capacity, support Canadian SMEs, and strengthen supply-chain resilience in critical photonics technologies. Global interest: Minister Joly confirmed that firms from Europe and Asia have already expressed keen interest in taking stakes or investing in the facility. She highlighted opportunities to build a “middle-power alliance” by deepening ties with these partners, complementing North American demand. Impact This initiative positions Canada as a leader in photonic semiconductors—a foundational technology for AI, quantum, and high-performance computing—at a time of global supply-chain pressures. By leveraging international capital while safeguarding Canadian sovereignty, the spin-out is expected to drive economic growth, productivity, and technological sovereignty. Canadian companies such as Ranovus have already signaled intent to participate as investors or operators."}}}, "rest_id": "2053583530693914977", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "22", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
"15 minutes of exposure to this can reduce blood glucose elevation by 27.7%."
— Journal of Biophotonics, 2024

"It activates the enzyme inside your cells that produces ATP, the energy currency of every living cell."
— Frontiers in Photonics, 2024

"It improves muscle recovery, accelerates wound healing, and supports tissue repair across dermatology, neurological function, and metabolic health."
— News Medical / Photobiomodulation review, 2026

All refer to red light.

The same wavelengths your great-grandparents got at sunrise and sunset every day.

The same wavelengths almost completely missing from modern indoor life.

Combined value: ~8 billion SEK. https://t.co/k9EMCinhs5 It is framed as these investors are driving this on speculation. Yes maybe, that is of course possible. Or is there another angle? My take, SEK8B..what? Hence my reaction would probably be the opposite, this could be worth investigating: What thesis are these allocators actually trading? Is it pure speculation with SEK 8B in play? Who sits on these SEK8B? EFN however have asked "Richard Schatz, senior researcher in photonics at RISE Research Institutes of Sweden and former consultant for Sivers", for his opinion about Sivers. So what did Mr. Richard Schatz say: "There are several companies that manufacture these kinds of lasers, and Sivers is far from alone. I don't understand why the company has become so hyped. There are many players, not just in Europe and the US, but also in China," says Schatz. Honestly I have been COO and CEO at Sivers for almost 10 years 2015-2024 (2015 we had 18 headcount I known everyone in the Company until 2024) and also ask Board members that was in the board many years before me, both for Sivers and former CST Global (Sivers Photonics today) and none of them can recollect Mr. Schatz. I also sent an email, asking the reporter that interviewed Mr S and asked when he claims to have been a consultant at Sivers. Without reply. This doesn't really matter, it is just shows where the digging stops. Sivers Semiconductors is one of a handful of merchant InP laser suppliers globally. They have disclosed partnerships with POET Technologies, Jabil (1.6T LRO), O-Net, Ayar Labs and operate in the External Light Source category that scale-up CPO architectures depend on. Either are all these US investors dramatically wrong about the photonics layer of AI infrastructure, or the institutional allocators routing through US brokerages have identified what most Swedish investors haven't yet. Maybe this is worth investigating which? To be 100% clear no one knows (not even me), who has the right hypothesis, that we might know in 2028-2030 time frame. To help anyone intressted, I made this free and open substack to explain how the money flows. As they said in Washington follow the money... text:
A great feature by MIT on Piris Lab, a Tekedia Capital portfolio company developing photonics systems to power the next-generation infrastructure of AI.

Largely, Piris Labs is building a full-stack AI inference platform designed to eliminate one of the most critical constraints in modern computing: the data movement bottleneck. By combining proprietary photonic hardware with a vertically integrated software stack, the company addresses the “memory wall” that limits the efficiency of today’s GPU-based systems.

Read here  https://t.co/0yykNVI8eL

Their approach delivers comparable performance to traditional compute clusters, at a significantly lower cost, by improving effective FLOP utilization and reducing latency. In doing so, Piris Labs is helping make the unit economics of trillion-parameter AI models truly sustainable.

Tekedia Capital >> we fund innovations

Largely, Piris Labs is building a full-stack AI inference platform designed to eliminate one of the most critical constraints in modern computing: the data movement bottleneck. By combining proprietary photonic hardware with a vertically integrated software stack, the company addresses the “memory wall” that limits the efficiency of today’s GPU-based systems. Read here https://t.co/0yykNVI8eL Their approach delivers comparable performance to traditional compute clusters, at a significantly lower cost, by improving effective FLOP utilization and reducing latency. In doing so, Piris Labs is helping make the unit economics of trillion-parameter AI models truly sustainable. text:
The 800G to 1.6T transition is mid-ramp now, accelerating into 2026 alongside Rubin.

Test and validation aren't optional. Every new module needs production qualification.

If silicon photonics is the wave, $VIAV is the toll booth most AI portfolios are missing.

$VIAV: $12B cap, 8.8x sales, P/E 40.

Pure-play photonics names like $LITE and $COHR trade richer because the market prices them as direct beneficiaries.

VIAV gets paid by every transceiver vendor regardless of which one wins the 1.6T design slot.

Silicon photonics is the chokepoint Nvidia's Rubin platform is built around. Copper is out, light is in across every AI server.

Inside that basket, $VIAV sits at the test layer. Every 1.6T transceiver shipped gets validated on their gear before production. https://t.co/ExNUXvx0mr

text:
The next $SIVE is probably in South Korea

I missed the $SIVE and the broader photonics run. By the time I finished my DD after seeing @aleabitoreddit's recommendation, it was already too late as it ran too high too fast.

Since then I have been scouring the market for similar opportunities that hasn't run as much as it did.

Specifically, I went hunting in Korea. Cheaper market, less analyst noise, and small-caps that the West hasn't gotten around to mispricing yet.

That's where I found OE Solutions. KOSDAQ ticker $138080.KQ. A Korean optical transceiver company that, the more I dug, kept giving me the strange feeling that I was reading an earlier version of the Sivers thesis--except this one already runs a fab at volume, ships product to named telecom customers, and trades at a fraction of the multiple.

A few things I now believe to be true after re-checking each line against primary sources where I could.

OE Solutions did about KRW 57.4bn in 2025 revenue (~$40m). Sivers reported SEK 304.1m in full-year 2025 revenue (~$30m). So the smaller-cap company does more revenue.

Market caps. As of early May 2026, Sivers sits at roughly SEK 15-16.5bn (~$1.5-1.7bn). OE Solutions sits at roughly KRW 458bn (~$340m at current FX). So OE has higher revenue at roughly one-fifth of Sivers' market cap. The price-to-sales gap is the headline.

Sell-side expects OE to swing to operating profit in 2026. Hana Securities is the most explicit on this--target raised from KRW 30,000 to KRW 50,000, "buy" rating maintained. Sivers, on consensus, is still loss-making in 2026. Treat OE's profit turn as sell-side opinion, not company guidance, but the directional gap is real.

Both companies make Indium Phosphide lasers, the enabling component for high-speed optical interconnects in AI data centers. The right framing isn't "chokepoint material"--it's that scaled InP suppliers are few, wafer yields are constrained, and demand is running ahead of capacity.

Where the two companies sit in the stack is different.

Sivers operates a 100mm InP wafer fab in Glasgow (acquired via CST Global in 2017). According to Sivers' own materials, the facility ships around 1 million lasers per month, with over 45 million lasers cumulatively in the field. They are not a small-scale supplier. To scale further for AI data center volumes, Sivers announced an OEM partnership with WIN Semiconductors in Taoyuan in March 2025--an outsourced foundry arrangement to expand DFB laser output. So the capacity story is "established volume supplier scaling further."

OE Solutions, per its own 2024 IR deck, runs vertical integration from epitaxy through chip fabrication to transceiver assembly out of its Gwangju facility. Korean industry reporting describes OE as mass-producing chips today and using its own laser diodes inside its own transceivers--with secondary reporting suggesting external chip sales to Chinese customers since 2024. I could not find a primary disclosure that puts an exact wafer-per-month number on the OE fab. So treat the comparison as: Sivers ships components into other people's modules, OE makes the modules itself. Different position in the stack, both with their own fabs.

Customers. Sivers has named, primary-source partnerships: Jabil for a 1.6T LRO transceiver collaboration announced April 15, 2026; POET Technologies for external light source modules, with prototypes targeted for H1 2026 and production-readiness for end-2026 (per Sivers' own Q4 2025 report, qualification with POET is ongoing); Ayar Labs going back to ECOC 2022; https://t.co/VKc0iGZ1CB in SATCOM. Most are real but development-stage.

OE Solutions is named in Korean industry reporting as supplying Samsung, Nokia, Ericsson, Cisco and Ciena, with NTT-bound FTTH volume coming through a Sumitomo handover. There are reports from Korea saying OE began mass-supplying 10G EPON OLT transceivers for NTT. I could not find a clean OE press release proving every one of those names is currently shipping, so the right characterization is "named in industry reporting as a customer of," not "verified invoice-paying." That said, the 2025 revenue jump of nearly 80% YoY needs to come from somewhere--it's not coming from thin air.

Both companies are positioned for the 1.6T transition. Sivers via the Jabil collaboration. OE via a 23 dBm cooled ELSFP module for CPO external light sources, with sampling targeted for Q3 2026 (this one is on OE's own blog as a primary source). Korean reporting also mentions a 1.6T OSFP unveiled at OFC 2026, but I couldn't surface OE's own press release for that exact product, so I'd hedge that line.

Sivers' stock is up roughly 1200%+ over the past twelve months. The thesis the market paid for is "InP laser scarcity, scaling capacity, AI data center exposure." That thesis worked.

The OE thesis rhymes. Same InP laser scarcity. Vertical integration that Sivers is now pursuing through outsourced partners. Earnings inflection driven by something more concrete than a hyperscaler win--the Sumitomo to NTT FTTH volume handover, which Korean industry reporting describes as roughly one-third reflected in current numbers, with the rest expected to flow through over the next several quarters. Treat that one-third figure as secondary reporting, not a number from a filing.

So why does the gap exist?

Sivers is European-listed and pursuing a Nasdaq dual listing--Western analyst coverage, English-language Substack writeups, the whole machinery. OE Solutions trades on KOSDAQ. The Korean small-cap discount is real and persistent. OE's investor disclosures page also shows repeated CB conversion entries through March-April 2026, which is exactly the dilution overhang you'd expect to weigh on the multiple.

These are real risks. I'm not waving them away.

But the valuation gap is too wide for the operational gap.

If OE Solutions gets even half of Sivers' price-to-sales multiple, applied to a revenue base that grows the way sell-side expects, you're looking at multiples of the current market cap. Pure rerating math. No new news required. And if the AI data center qualifications already in flight start landing--ITLA modules with Photon Bridge, the ELSFP sample shipments, and whatever materializes at the higher data rates--the rerating compounds.

The asymmetry is what matters. The Sumitomo handover and the laser diode internalization carry the stock to a meaningful re-rate without anything heroic happening. The data center catalyst is a free option on top of that. If it hits, it hits the way Sivers did. If it doesn't, the earnings inflection still pays you.

I missed Sivers. At Korean market open, I opened a position in OE Solutions.

Not financial advice. Do your own due diligence.

text:
deep dive:

The tech is real. The management is radioactive. Here's why this is the most important bear case in photonics right now.

POET Technologies makes optical interposers — silicon wafers that let you snap photonic components into place without manual alignment. That's a 75% cost reduction on the most expensive part of building optical transceivers. If it works at scale, it's a real bottleneck-solver for AI data centers.

The problem isn't the engineering. It's everything else.

Revenue: $1.07 million trailing twelve months. That's not a typo. Q4 revenue was $341,202 — all prototype fees, zero product revenue. The company lost $42.7 million that same quarter. EBITDA is negative $39 million.

Yet the market cap is over $1 billion. That's 700x+ EV/revenue. For context, Marvell trades at 15-20x, Lumentum at 9-15x, Coherent at 9x. POET is priced like a scaled platform company that doesn't exist yet.

The cash position is real — $430 million after massive dilution (they raised $525 million in the last two quarters). So no bankruptcy risk. But the dilution story is brutal: they funded a pre-revenue R&D lab by printing shares, which created the PFIC tax problem.

Here's where it gets ugly. On April 14, Wolfpack Research published a short report flagging POET as a likely Passive Foreign Investment Company under US tax law — punitive tax treatment for American shareholders. The CFO called it a "nothing burger" on a YouTube livestream.

Two days later, he publicly disclosed Marvell's confidential purchase orders on that same YouTube channel. Marvell formally canceled all contracts on April 23, citing breach of NDA. That was POET's most validated commercial pipeline — gone in 48 hours because the CFO couldn't stop talking to retail stock promoters.

The stock dropped 47% on the cancellation news. A federal class action lawsuit was filed April 29. Insider selling hit $400K in the 90 days before the collapse.

Risks: PFIC tax status unresolved (if confirmed, institutional capital flees). Management credibility destroyed — LITEON and Luxshare are now watching a partner who leaked Marvell's supply chain data. Malaysian manufacturing ramp at Globetronics is unproven — passive alignment yields at volume are the key assumption, and they haven't demonstrated it yet. Class action litigation is ongoing.

The technology has genuine acquisition value — the patent portfolio on wafer-level optical testing is defensible, and the ferroelectric memory research (published in Nature Communications, co-authored by the CEO) addresses the AI memory wall. But the equity vehicle is toxic at any price above distressed multiples.

Verdict: SELL — brilliant IP trapped inside a governance disaster. Watch the May 13 earnings call: if the board doesn't fire the CFO, it signals terminal corporate rot.

@WolfpackRes called this one out.

Wasn't there some advancement relatively recently on doing photonics within silicon using modern manufacturing techniques? May have been a year or two ago but I swear I remember reading something along those lines. Also vaguely remember reading something about cheaply laser etching data holographically in regular borosillicate glass media too, maybe out of Meta, or actually I think it was Microsoft?

Idk, I feel like all the pieces are there for this to happen technologically, you'd think somebody out there has to be throwing some decent money at it

$AAOI 

According to the Management, target of... almost $500 MM in Revenue per month from mid-2027 on 😱🔥🔥

Clearly supply-demand situation in Optics/Photonics Networking is so ultra tight, the only question to get to that target is.... can they execute?

$AAOI

AND

They raised that projection to $471/m for mid-2027 (not including CATV)

To put it in context, they just did ~$150m in QUARTERLY revenue

text:
Top 4 photonics + asymmetric 10 bagger setups for the next decade:

$SIVE - Sivers Semiconductors
Building the optical backbone for AI datacenters. Tiny market cap with exposure to co-packaged optics, laser arrays, and next-gen AI interconnects. One of the more underfollowed photonics plays in Europe.

$LWLG - Lightwave Logic
Electro-optic polymers that could drastically reduce power consumption and latency in AI networking. If polymers become part of future silicon photonics stacks, upside is massive.

$ALMU - Aeluma
Compound semiconductors + photonics on silicon. Positioned at the intersection of sensing, quantum, AI, and defense. Very early but huge optionality.

$WOLF - Wolfspeed
Silicon carbide infrastructure for the electrification and AI power era. High risk balance sheet, but if execution improves the rerating potential is enormous.

Wildcard asymmetric names:

$MRAM - Everspin Technologies
Persistent memory for AI, aerospace, industrial, and defense systems. Small niche today but strong long-term asymmetric potential.

$ALRIB - Riber
Advanced epitaxy systems critical for compound semiconductors, photonics, and future semiconductor infrastructure.

$LPK - LPKF Laser & Electronics
Precision laser manufacturing and advanced semiconductor packaging exposure. Strong positioning if photonics and advanced chip packaging accelerate.

$SVCO - Silvaco
EDA/software infrastructure play for semiconductor design, power electronics, and emerging photonics workflows. Underfollowed picks-and-shovels angle on the semiconductor cycle.

text:
$AEVA is no longer just a LiDAR story.

The market is still valuing it like a speculative auto sensor company while management is quietly positioning for the next AI infrastructure wave:

• Silicon photonics
• Optical interconnects
• AI data center bandwidth bottlenecks
• Semiconductor manufacturing
• Defense autonomy
• Smart infrastructure
• Industrial robotics
This earnings call changed the narrative.
Key points the market is missing:
→ Nikon commercial deployment is LIVE
→ Shipping 1,000+ industrial sensors already
→ Semiconductor equipment customers using AEVA sensors in fabs
→ NVIDIA DRIVE Hyperion reference LiDAR outside China
→ Defense expansion with Forterra
→ Data center optical interconnect commentary now entering the story
The important part:
AEVA’s core competency isn’t “car LiDAR.”
It’s high-performance silicon photonics + FMCW sensing.
That has applications FAR beyond automotive.

The AI capex cycle is creating massive demand for:
• optical transport
• photonics
• machine perception
• edge AI sensing

And AEVA is now sitting at the intersection of all four.

Still early.
Still speculative.
But this is evolving from a niche EV sensor play into a broader Physical AI + AI photonics platform story.
That’s where rerating potential comes from.

text:
Update on tonight's $COHR print:

Revenue: +20.5% YoY, beat by 1.7%
EPS: $1.41 (missed $1.43 by $0.02)
Q4 guide: $1.98B mid (in-line)
After-hours: -9%

That's THREE photonics names in 48 hours that beat and sold off:
$LITE -8%
$OUST -11%
$COHR -9%

Multiple compression isn't just on premium names anymore. It's the whole sector.

My framework update:
Base case shifted from 3-7 day pullback (was 45% prob) to 8-14 days (now 45% prob).

The thesis isn't broken. The trade is just exhausted at these multiples.

$AAOI tomorrow now needs more than a beat. Needs a SIGNIFICANT guide raise above $1.1B to spark relief.

NFA. DYOR.

text:
Like many other FinX folks, I took a small position in $SIVE. This is one of my favorite speculative plays at the moment at ~3% of my portfolio, but….

I am keeping my eye on how well the 130 person org can juggle 5G mmWave + AI-DC photonics + LIDAR + SATCOM beamformers + defense + Nasdaq dual-listing prep + audit uplift. 

They have a lot of moving parts at the moment for a relatively small team ($LITE has 10k+ headcount for context). Perfect execution is imperative to maintain the 3B+ MC thesis. 

Right now, I still love this play at <50SEK

text:
The technical setup for POET Technologies $POET  at $10.95 reflects a stock that is currently one of the most explosive "AI-Photonics" plays in the market. Following a multi-year basing period, POET has entered a high-conviction Strong Bullish Alignment, recently surging on massive institutional volume.

Here is the strategic breakdown of the "Optical Interposer" pivot and the technical setup for the week ahead.

📊 Technical Health Check: The "Breakaway" Gap
The system is flashing Bullish Alignment, with the price now trading in a "Power Trend" above both the Weekly Kumo and the Daily EMA.

Weekly Macro Trend: POET has successfully cleared the "Cloud of Death" that capped its price throughout 2024–2025. The Weekly Kijun-sen (purple) at $9.60 has officially flipped from resistance to a primary floor. As long as the stock stays above $9.50 on a weekly close, the macro bull case is the dominant narrative.
The Volume Profile: On May 8, POET surged 14.3% on over 70 million shares—more than triple its average daily volume. In technical analysis, this "Volume Spike + Price Breakout" is the ultimate confirmation that institutional accumulation is occurring.
The Signal Success: The Long Signal from April 20 at $8.59 is now up +27.5%. This trade was a "Macro Match" from day one, proving that the system correctly identified the shift in AI data center demand toward optical interconnects.

🧬 Fundamental Catalyst: 800G Scale-Up & Q1 Earnings

The $10.95 price point reflects the market's realization that POET is transitioning from a "research lab" to a "high-volume manufacturer."

Q1 Earnings (Thursday, May 14, 2026): POET is scheduled to report after the close this Thursday. While analysts expect a loss of $0.04/share, the market is solely focused on the Revenue Guidance and Backlog.

The $5M+ "Infinity" Order: Earlier this year, POET received a production order exceeding $5M for its 800G Infinity optical engines. Management has guided for delivery in H2 2026, and investors want to see if this has expanded.
The 1.6T Partnership (LITEON/Lessengers): POET is currently co-developing 1.6T and 3.2T optical modules. Any update on prototypes for these next-generation AI speeds (expected late 2026) would likely send the stock back toward its 52-week high of $15.50.
Manufacturing Readiness: High-volume production in Malaysia is slated to begin in Q2 (now). Any confirmation that the facility is "live" and shipping light-source products is the primary "Go/No-Go" signal for the bulls.

💡 Hybrid System Conclusion
The Bottom Line:  $POET is the "AI Bottleneck Buster." >The technicals are in "Strong Bullish" alignment because POET's technology addresses the #1 problem in AI data centers: Heat and Latency. 
The 70M volume day is a definitive market "vote" that this tech is real.
Trading Rule: With a massive 27% gain already in the bag, the risk of a "sell the news" event on Thursday is high. Do not chase the $11.00 level. Instead, look for a "Buy the Dip" opportunity if the stock pullbacks to the $9.50–$9.80 support zone (the Daily EMA). As long as it holds above $9.50, the trend is up.
Final Thought: We follow the photons, not the hype. POET has $430M in cash to bridge the gap to 2027 profitability.  Manufacturing scale-up is the current reality for $POET.

Summary: Resistance is at $12.30; Support is at $9.60. Thursday's call will determine if POET is a "Niche Component Maker" or the "Standard Interconnect" for the next generation of AI clusters.

This isn’t a rookie team trying to figure things out for the first time. These are people who’ve already been part of scaling semiconductor and deep-tech companies before. Chairman Bami Bastani has 40+ years in the industry. Former CEO and board member of ANADIGICS, Trident Microsystems and Meru Networks, and later SVP at $GFS where he led the high-growth Mobile & Wireless Infrastructure business. CEO Vickram Vathulya previously led Nuvotronics through a strategic and operational transformation focused on long-term growth and value creation. Before that he revitalized Maxim Integrated’s largest and most profitable business unit and helped scale RF/wireless businesses at Maxim and $NXP. Karin Raj brings heavyweight telecom and networking experience from $NOK, Ericsson and Huawei, with previous CTO and product leadership roles across the industry. Todd Thomson adds major financial and strategic firepower from Citigroup, GE Capital and Bain & Co. MD Wireless Harish Krishnaswamy brings world-class mmWave expertise and experience around strategic semiconductor funding opportunities like the CHIPS Act. On the photonics side, Andrew McKee brings deep technical and manufacturing expertise, while advisor Ian McCann has helped execute multi-billion-dollar semiconductor deals at Dialog, Linear and $ADI. CFO Heine Thorsgaard has spent 20+ years driving growth, operational efficiency and long-term value creation across technology companies, most recently as CFO of Napatech. And CRO Raymond Biagan has a strong track record of driving major revenue growth and customer expansion across the semiconductor industry, including at $GFS. Great technology alone doesn’t build billion-dollar companies. Execution does. text:
10. What is the market still missing?

In my opinion, the market is still looking too much at what $SIVE is today and not enough at what could happen if several ramps start layering in at the same time.

No one knows exactly how photonics, pluggables and CPO will develop, but I believe it will shock a lot of people how big it ultimately becomes. The market has a hard time grasping the scale before everything really kicks off. But once it does, the company could already be valued at multiples far beyond where we are today.

Jabil validation, WIN scaling, pipeline growth, institutional interest and Nasdaq path are lining up.

But if even a few of these ramps hit, I think the market may have to rethink what kind of company $SIVE is becoming.

$SIVE has been grinding for years. Now everything is starting to line up at once.

To me, this no longer looks like a small speculative semiconductor story. It looks like a company that may be entering the steep part of the curve just as several massive infrastructure markets begin accelerating at the same time.

And I still think the market is early.

What other questions around $SIVE should we dig into next?

text:
9. What are the biggest risks?

The risks are real: CPO adoption can take longer, volumes can slip, dilution exists, execution must be proven.

But the multi-market setup gives downside protection. If AI photonics takes longer, wireless, SATCOM, LiDAR and defense can still drive growth.

text:
6. Is this only an AI photonics story?

No, and that is exactly what many people miss.

AI photonics is the headline, but there are several other shots on goal. The same strong III-V platform and team is now showing up across AI, LiDAR, SATCOM, telecom and defense.

Personally, the F100 consumer electronics/biosensing angle is still the joker for me. I’ve been waiting on that one for years. If that lands properly, I genuinely believe it could take us to a 2.5B+ market cap overnight. Long-cycle stuff, yes, but qualification progress still seems to be moving forward.

text:
4. Why is the WIN Semiconductor partnership so important?

Because scaling photonics is not just about technology. It’s about manufacturing.

$SIVE chose an asset-light model. Glasgow for pilot production and control. WIN Semiconductor as the high-volume foundry path.

To me, WIN is the cost-down and scaling engine behind the entire photonics story. This is what separates the companies that can actually deliver at volume from those that stay small.

text:
Never ever FOMO. If you look hard enough, there are always new opportunities to be found.

Glass, photonics, CPUs, NeoClouds, power systems, memory, and everything semis that touches the sun will go up.

If the consensus now is that memory is simply safe to invest in and is the most commonly agreed-upon bottleneck, then the market will do the opposite in the short term, creating a new opportunity, most likely on macro bad news.

Money rewards those who are early and right (i.e. glass substrate) or patient and just on time (i.e. memory).

text:
Optical transceiver and silicon photonics packaging is the actual chokepoint in the AI infrastructure stack that the index trade misses.

Each AI cluster needs hundreds of thousands of high-bandwidth optical interconnects between racks, and packaging capacity is concentrated in a handful of Taiwanese names with multi-year qualified-vendor lead times.

The pricing power lives one step downstream of cell production where Foxconn-backed assemblers sit.

text:
$SIVE old news still very bullish 

Photonics AI interconnects target to $1.1 billion by 2028

Volume production expected to ramp by 2027 https://t.co/QDBFzmdrE6

The 10x Thesis: Identifying the Bottlenecks of the Next Decade $LWLG $SIVE $ALRIB $LPK $INFQ

This portfolio targets the "Picks and Shovels" of the fourth industrial revolution: AI infrastructure, Silicon Photonics, and next-gen Semiconductors. 

These companies aren't just market participants; they provide the critical hardware and software bridges that industry giants must cross to succeed.

1. $LWLG (Lightwave Logic)

The Bottleneck: Data centers are hitting a "speed and heat wall." Traditional silicon modulators are too slow and consume too much power for future AI needs.

The Edge: Their organic polymers enable data transmission at speeds exceeding 800G and 1.6T with significantly lower power. If this becomes the industry standard, LWLG replaces legacy photonics.

2. $SIVE (Sivers Semiconductors)

The Bottleneck: Massive AI clusters require seamless optical chip-to-chip communication, but laser sources are difficult to integrate.

The Edge: Their CW-WDM (Continuous Wave Wavelength Division Multiplexing) lasers are the "engine" for optical computing. 

3. $ALRIB (Riber SA)

The Bottleneck: You cannot build advanced compound semiconductors (like those for 5G or LiDAR) without atom-by-atom precision.

The Edge: Riber is the world leader in Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) machines. They are the gatekeepers; without their hardware, the manufacturers of specialized chips cannot exist.

4. $LPK (LPKF Laser &Electronics SE)

The Bottleneck: Organic chip substrates melt under the heat of high-end AI processors. The industry is pivoting to glass.

The Edge: Their LIDE (Laser Induced Deep Etching) technology is the only proven method to process glass-core substrates at scale. LPKF owns the key to the next generation of chip packaging.

5. $INFQ (Infleqtion)

The Bottleneck: Classical supercomputers cannot solve complex quantum physics or advanced encryption.

The Edge: Focusing on "Cold Atom" technology, they provide the hardware and software stack to make quantum computing commercial. They are building the infrastructure for a post-classical computing world.

6. $SPIR (Spire Global)

The Bottleneck: Real-time global data (weather, maritime, aviation) is often fragmented or unavailable in remote areas.

The Edge: Spire operates one of the largest Nano-Satellite constellations. They offer "Space-as-a-Service," providing high-margin, recurring data subscriptions for the global economy.

7. $WOLF (Wolfspeed)

The Bottleneck: Traditional silicon chips are inefficient for high-power applications like EV drivetrains and fast chargers.

The Edge: The global leader in Silicon Carbide (SiC). SiC chips increase EV range and reduce charging times. Wolfspeed owns the essential material for the global transition to electricity.

8. $OUST (Ouster)

The Bottleneck: For robots and autonomous vehicles to operate safely, they need to "see" in 3D with high resolutio, cameras alone aren't enough.

The Edge: By moving from analog to Digital LiDAR, Ouster has simplified the hardware into a single chip.
This makes their sensors cheaper, more reliable, and easier to integrate into "Physical AI."

9. $SVCO (Silvaco) 

The Bottleneck: Designing next-gen chips (Power, Photonics, Atomic-scale) requires specialized simulation software that standard tools can't provide.

The Edge: Silvaco provides TCAD (Technology Computer-Aided Design) software. They are a "bottleneck within a bottleneck", engineers at companies like $TSM or $NXP use it to design chips that drive the portfolio. 

10. $MRAM (Everspin Technologies)

The Bottleneck: Standard RAM is "volatile", it loses all data during a power failure, which is a disaster for industrial AI and data centers.

The Edge: They are the only commercial provider of MRAM (Magnetoresistive RAM). It is as fast as RAM but keeps data without power. It is the "forever memory" for critical AI infrastructure.

The 10x Goal: While these companies have smaller market caps, they hold the IP and market position required by giants like NVIDIA, Tesla, or Amazon. If even one of these "bottleneck" technologies becomes the global standard, the upside is exponential.

What's your favorite stock?

Not financial advice.

These companies aren't just market participants; they provide the critical hardware and software bridges that industry giants must cross to succeed. 1. $LWLG (Lightwave Logic) The Bottleneck: Data centers are hitting a "speed and heat wall." Traditional silicon modulators are too slow and consume too much power for future AI needs. The Edge: Their organic polymers enable data transmission at speeds exceeding 800G and 1.6T with significantly lower power. If this becomes the industry standard, LWLG replaces legacy photonics. 2. $SIVE (Sivers Semiconductors) The Bottleneck: Massive AI clusters require seamless optical chip-to-chip communication, but laser sources are difficult to integrate. The Edge: Their CW-WDM (Continuous Wave Wavelength Division Multiplexing) lasers are the "engine" for optical computing. 3. $ALRIB (Riber SA) The Bottleneck: You cannot build advanced compound semiconductors (like those for 5G or LiDAR) without atom-by-atom precision. The Edge: Riber is the world leader in Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) machines. They are the gatekeepers; without their hardware, the manufacturers of specialized chips cannot exist. 4. $LPK (LPKF Laser &Electronics SE) The Bottleneck: Organic chip substrates melt under the heat of high-end AI processors. The industry is pivoting to glass. The Edge: Their LIDE (Laser Induced Deep Etching) technology is the only proven method to process glass-core substrates at scale. LPKF owns the key to the next generation of chip packaging. 5. $INFQ (Infleqtion) The Bottleneck: Classical supercomputers cannot solve complex quantum physics or advanced encryption. The Edge: Focusing on "Cold Atom" technology, they provide the hardware and software stack to make quantum computing commercial. They are building the infrastructure for a post-classical computing world. 6. $SPIR (Spire Global) The Bottleneck: Real-time global data (weather, maritime, aviation) is often fragmented or unavailable in remote areas. The Edge: Spire operates one of the largest Nano-Satellite constellations. They offer "Space-as-a-Service," providing high-margin, recurring data subscriptions for the global economy. 7. $WOLF (Wolfspeed) The Bottleneck: Traditional silicon chips are inefficient for high-power applications like EV drivetrains and fast chargers. The Edge: The global leader in Silicon Carbide (SiC). SiC chips increase EV range and reduce charging times. Wolfspeed owns the essential material for the global transition to electricity. 8. $OUST (Ouster) The Bottleneck: For robots and autonomous vehicles to operate safely, they need to "see" in 3D with high resolutio, cameras alone aren't enough. The Edge: By moving from analog to Digital LiDAR, Ouster has simplified the hardware into a single chip. This makes their sensors cheaper, more reliable, and easier to integrate into "Physical AI." 9. $SVCO (Silvaco) The Bottleneck: Designing next-gen chips (Power, Photonics, Atomic-scale) requires specialized simulation software that standard tools can't provide. The Edge: Silvaco provides TCAD (Technology Computer-Aided Design) software. They are a "bottleneck within a bottleneck", engineers at companies like $TSM or $NXP use it to design chips that drive the portfolio. 10. $MRAM (Everspin Technologies) The Bottleneck: Standard RAM is "volatile", it loses all data during a power failure, which is a disaster for industrial AI and data centers. The Edge: They are the only commercial provider of MRAM (Magnetoresistive RAM). It is as fast as RAM but keeps data without power. It is the "forever memory" for critical AI infrastructure. The 10x Goal: While these companies have smaller market caps, they hold the IP and market position required by giants like NVIDIA, Tesla, or Amazon. If even one of these "bottleneck" technologies becomes the global standard, the upside is exponential. What's your favorite stock? When markets take a breather after a melt-up, watch the big options writers 🌊🐻

For example, in $LITE a ~$13M call selling position opened on the 700 strike on Friday 💰

Two trading days later, the stock is down over 10% and those 700 calls are still ITM and open

Gigaphoton⁠�
Litografiya üçün excimer lazerlər. Çip istehsalında əsas oyunçulardan biridir.

Dünyanın ən güclü fotonika şirkətlərindən biri.
Lazer sensorları, fotodetektorlar, elmi optika, tibbi və kvant texnologiyaları hazırlayır.
Nidec⁠�
Yüksək dəqiqlikli motor və optik sistemlərdə istifadə olunan texnologiyalar hazırlayır.
Mitsubishi Electric⁠�
Sənaye lazerləri, fiber lazerlər və fabrik avtomatlaşdırılması.
Panasonic Holdings⁠�
Optik və lazer əsaslı istehsal sistemləri üzərində işləyir.
Canon⁠� və Nikon⁠�
Litografiya və ultra dəqiq optik sistemlər.

Right now:

text:
BAM
$TSEM — the only foundry on Earth with a production-grade silicon photonics PDK, ~80% SiPho market share, and NVDA, Broadcom, Marvell, Coherent and Lumentum all depend on them. $920M capex funded 70%+ by customer prepayments takes capacity 5x by Q4 2026. @tommyleed8888 I wasn't aware

If I already thought it would do it under normal conditions, now even more so

But this is almost certainly due to a widespread rally across the entire photonics sector

And especially a short term trend reversal in $SIVE

text:
New review from Gassab & Craddock in J. Phys.: Photonics ... "Optical properties of the cytoskeleton", provides a comprehensive analysis of light-matter interactions in microtubules from the biological side: exciton transport, Fano resonances, superradiance, waveguiding, and the non-Hermitian Hamiltonian formalism for tryptophan networks.

Interestingly, this is directly complementary to my recent paper on geometric foundations for collective quantum phenomena in microtubules. They approach from quantum optics and biophysics ... what does the formalism predict for these biological structures? I approach from nanophotonics ... what do engineered systems with matching geometry actually do?

The convergence ... Their "decisive tests" call for "perturbations that alter bath coupling while leaving geometry approximately fixed", which is what my Prediction 3 (anesthetic perturbation) proposes. Their non-Hermitian framework partitions tryptophan modes into superradiant and subradiant channels, which maps directly onto the non-Hermitian dynamics I identify as a shared geometric feature between engineered nanophotonic platforms and microtubules.

Two independent approaches. Same structure. Same experimental priorities.
That's what convergence looks like.

Optical properties of the cytoskeleton: a review of light–matter interactions, quantum effects, and biological implications
https://t.co/uLCZEuxOdG

Geometric Foundations for Collective Quantum Phenomena in Microtubules: Convergent Evidence from Nanophotonic Analogs
https://t.co/vtlNPORoNW

text:
Deadline Extended for IPC 2026 Submissions!
There’s still time to be part of the IEEE Photonics Conference (IPC) 2026 — the flagship meeting of the IEEE Photonics Society.

New Submission Deadline: 18 May 2026

IPC 2026 brings together global leaders across academia and industry to share the latest advancements in photonics, including:
• Optical AI & Computational Photonics
• Quantum Photonics
• Optical Communications & Networks
• Biophotonics & Medical Optics
• And more across a broad technical program

8–12 November 2026 | Denver, Colorado, USA
Don’t miss your chance to present your work, connect with peers, and contribute to the future of photonics.

Submit your abstract & learn more: https://t.co/glSW1REzW6
